title: Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa
intro: Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa, commonly known as red alder, is a deciduous tree species native to Europe and North Africa, characterized by its conical shape and grayish-brown bark. The leaves, bark, and roots of red alder have been used in traditional medicine for their potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral properties, which may help to alleviate respiratory issues, wounds, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of red alder include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenes, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with red alder include decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds, and to promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa is a variant of Alnus glutinosa, commonly known as the black alder. It belongs to the family Betulaceae, which consists of deciduous trees and shrubs. Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa is a member of the genus Alnus, a group of trees that are known for their conical shapes and conical catkins. This variant is native to Europe and Asia, where it grows in wetland areas and along rivers. Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa is often found in association with other trees, such as the alder buckthorn and the common alder.

constituents: Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa is known to contain various active constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and tannins. These compounds are responsible for its potential medicinal properties and antioxidant effects. The plant's extracts have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. Additionally, Alnus glutinosa lusus rugosa may also contain other bioactive compounds such as terpenoids and saponins. Further research is needed to fully understand the composition and potential uses of this plant's active constituents.
